coredata.dc:title: Langmuir-Blodgett assembly of visible light responsive TiO2 nanotube arrays/graphene oxide heterostructure
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Ying
Gao, Hongyan
Wei, Danming
Dong, Xinju
Cao, Yan
coredata.dc:description: The hybrid nanocomposites of titanium dioxide (TiO2) with graphene oxide (GO) have recently garnered much attention as electronic devices, energy conversion devices, photocatalysts and other applications. In this study, Langmuir-Blodgett (LB) assembly method was firstly reported to prepare a TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNA)-GO heterostructure. The as-prepared TNA-GO sample was characterized by X-ray diffraction, Raman spectra, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The promising characteristics of this TNA-GO material, the inexpensive, nontoxic and highly visible-light responsiveness, may raise the potential uses in many, various photocatalytic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2 nanotube arrays
Graphene oxide
Langmuir-Blodgett assembly
Photoresponse

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Office of Experimental Program to Stimulate Competitive Research

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the 2014-2015 NSF RSP EPSCoR program and 2015-2017 NSF REG & RSP EPSCoR program (the National Science Foundation under Cooperative Agreement No. 1355438), and the support from the NSF-CHE-MRI under the Award ID of 1338072. Authors also extend our acknowledgement to partial support by the U.S. Department of Agriculture ( 5040-12630-004-00D ). Additionally, the authors also very much appreciate the help from Dr. Sanju Gupta ( sanju.gupta@wku.edu ) - Raman tests, Dr. John Andersland (john.andersland@wku.edu) - electron microscope training, Dr. Hanjing Tian ( htian@mail.wvu.edu ) - XPS tests, and Mr. Craig Clark ( william.clark927@topper.wku.edu ) - proofreading.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow chart for the fabrication of TNA-GO.

50,000 times magnification SEM images from top view of (a) TNA and (b) TNA-GO. (c) 5000 and (d) 10,000 times magnification SEM images of TNA from side view. (e) 10,000 and (f) 100,000 times magnification TEM images of TNA. EDX analysis of (g) TNA and (h) TNA-GO.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) Raman spectra of TNA and TNA-GO.
(a) XPS whole survey spectrum of TNA and TNA-GO. The high-resolution (b) C 1s and (c) O 1s XPS spectra of TNA and TNA-GO.

(a) UV–vis DRS and (b) relationship between (Ahν)1/2 and hν. −Nyquist plots of the TNA and TNA-GO samples (c) without light exposure and (d) with light exposure. Transient photocurrent responses of TNA and TNA-GO with 50s visible light on-off cycles (λ>400nm) tested on the (e) 1st day and (f) 61st day. (g) Dark current of TNA and TNA-GO. Photograph depicting the hydrophilic surface of (h) TNA and (i) TNA-GO.

head.ce:title: Langmuir-Blodgett assembly of visible light responsive TiO2 nanotube arrays/graphene oxide heterostructure

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Combustion Science and Environmental Technology, Department of Chemistry, Western Kentucky University, Bowling Green, KY 42101, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Combustion Science and Environmental Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The hybrid nanocomposites of titanium dioxide (TiO2) with graphene oxide (GO) have recently garnered much attention as electronic devices, energy conversion devices, photocatalysts and other applications. In this study, Langmuir-Blodgett (LB) assembly method was firstly reported to prepare a TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNA)-GO heterostructure. The as-prepared TNA-GO sample was characterized by X-ray diffraction, Raman spectra, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The promising characteristics of this TNA-GO material, the inexpensive, nontoxic and highly visible-light responsiveness, may raise the potential uses in many, various photocatalytic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Titanium dioxide (TiO2), as a nontoxic, stable, less costly, and highly efficient photocatalyst, has wide applications in photovoltaics [1], dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) [2], hydrogen generation [3], solar fuel producing [4], pollution photo-digestion and other photocatalytic reactions. Due to its superb properties and abundant applications, various ways have been practiced to synthesize photocatalytic TiO2, i.e. sol-gel method [5], hydrothermal method [6], chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [7], liquid phase deposition (LPD) [8], etc. For the first time in 1999, Zwilling et al. reported the anodic oxidation method to fabricate highly ordered TiO2 nanostructured arrays (TNA) on titanium foil in a HF electrolyte [9], which has been followed by considerable research works since then. Compared to the aforementioned preparation methods, anodic oxidation of anodization TNA is not only an easy and convenient way, but also offers a strong mechanical strength directly on titanium substrates which can reduce the recombination of intrinsic electron−hole pairs to some degree [10]. Moreover, a relatively large surface to volume ratio of TNA brings different bonding characteristics on the surface, and eventually different properties from the thin film or bulk TiO2 [11].

However, TiO2 materials also have their own limitations as photocatalysts. Their band gap is 3.2eV, leading it responsive only to UV light which is only about 3–5% of the total solar energy. Moreover, its intrinsic electron–hole pair recombination is relatively high. To overcome this disadvantage, a series of TiO2 based nanocomposites have been co-synthesized with Ag [12], Au [13], ZnO [14] and other materials. Graphene with a two dimensional structure has been widely studied in materials science recently for its excellent properties, such as large surface to mass ratio, high mechanical strength (second- and third-order elastic stiffness of 340 Nm−1 and −690 Nm−1, breaking strength of 42 Nm−1) [15], great charge-carrier mobility (105cm2/Vs at an ambient temperature and 106cm2/Vs at low temperatures [16]), excellent thermal conductivity (up to ∼5000W/mK for the suspended single-layer graphene at room temperature [16]) and ease of functionalization [17]. Graphene oxide (GO), with graphene domains, defects, and oxygen-containing groups on the surface of graphene sheets, is also popular. It can offer many reactive handles for surface-modification reactions to form functionalized GO-based, or reduced graphene-based materials. Very recently, different types of TiO2/graphene, or TiO2/GO composites have been reported [18], in which TiO2 received light to excite photo-electrons, and the graphene can capture electrons and transport them out of circuit. These improved TiO2 composite can be readily applied in efficient photocatalytic utilizations.

Various methods have been used to prepare TiO2/graphene and TiO2/GO composites, i.e. sol-gel method [19], hydrothermal method [18]h, UV-assisted photoreduction method [18]j, microwave-assisted synthesis, etc [20]. Compared to the mentioned preparation methods, Langmuir–Blodgett (LB) assembly and deposition is a convenient and inexpensive method that can fabricate nano-films on solid substrate in a continuous manner over a large area. However, to our knowledge, no publications have been found up to this point using the LB assembly to prepare the co-photocatalysts. This study reports an easy and convenient way, using LB trough, to spread GO monolayers on TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNA) substrates and finally obtain a hybrid TNA-GO composite. Sufficient characterization methods have been applied for assisting in presenting an elaborate discussion.

All chemicals in this study were of analytical grade and used without any further purification. Among them, sodium nitrate (NaNO3, extra pure), and ammonium fluoride (NH4F; 98+%, extra pure) were purchased from Acros Organics Corporation. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2; 30%), graphite powder, potassium permanganate (KMnO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), methanol, ethanol, acetone and ethylene glycol were all purchased from Fisher Scientific Corporation. Titanium foil (Ti; ca. 99.7%) was purchased from STREM Chemicals. Throughout this study, deionized water (DI water) was always used.

GO was synthesized from graphite using a modified Hummers method [21]. First, 1g of graphite powder and 1g of NaNO3 were put into 45mL of concentrated H2SO4. Then, 6g of KMnO4 was gradually added into the solution with vigorous stirring in an ice bath to keep the temperature of the mixture below 30°C. After 10min, the ice bath was switched to a water bath, in which the mixture was stirred at 35°C for 4h. After the color of the mixture turned to dark brown, 200mL of deionized water was slowly dipped into the mixture. After continuously stirring for 1h, the water bath was switched to oil bath and the mixture was then stirred at 98°C for 2 more hours. Then, 30mL of 30% H2O2 was slowly added to the mixture. When the color of the mixture turned fully to brown, the mixture was transferred to a large beaker and washed to remove other ions using DI water.

The highly ordered TiO2 arrays was synthesized by an anodization approach. Prior to the anodization, the 3cm×1cm titanium foils were respectively pretreated in acetone, ethanol, and deionized water using ultrasonic cleaning, and then air dried at 60°C. Then, the anodic oxidization was performed in a two-electrode system with the pre-washed titanium foil as anode, a platinum (Pt) sheet as cathode and ethylene glycol solution containing 0.5wt% NH4F and 2 vol% DI water as electrolyte at room temperature. The applied potential was held at a constant 60V for 2h. After the anodization, the TNA sample was cleaned with deionized water and ethanol successively, and then air dried. After that, the prepared TNA sample was annealed in air at 500°C for 2h after a heating rate of 5°C/min.

GO films were assembled on TNA using the published Langmuir–Blodgett (LB) method [22]. To be specific, a DI water/methanol mixture with an optimal ratio of 1:5 was chosen as a solvent to spread GO monolayers rapidly onto the water surface in LB though (Kibron Inc., MicroTroughXS). Using an accurate glass syringe, 5mL of the GO dispersion was slowly dropped on the DI water surface at a speed of 100μL/min. When all GO dispersion was made on the surface, the pretreated TNA foil was raised by the vertical micromotor of the LB trough at a speed of 10mm/min, while two barriers were moving from both sides to the center at the same speed. The surface pressure was monitored by a sensor to record any movement. After the deposition, the substrate was dried in air overnight for 12h to stabilize the adhesion of GO layer to TNA. The GO deposition was repeated for 5 times to get the final TNA-GO composite.

For each sample, X-ray diffraction (XRD; ARL™ X'TRA Powder Diffractometer, Cu Kα radiation, λ=1.5406Å) was recorded at room temperature with a scan step of 0.02, a scanning speed of 0.2s/step. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Model JSM-6510LV, JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM; Model JEM-1400Plus, JOEL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) were used to characterize the morphologies and size of the synthesized samples. Their chemical composition were investigated by the energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). Raman spectra of samples were collected using a DXR Raman microscope (Thermo DXR), including a 780nm excitation laser and a confocal microscope. The Fourier transform infrared spectra were recorded by FT-IR Spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Spectrum Two) from 4000 to 500cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed with a multi–functional photoelectron spectrometer (Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos) using Al Ka radiation. UV–vis diffuse-reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) spectra were recorded with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Cary, 100 Bio) at wavelengths in the range of 200–800nm, with a baseline corrected by barium sulfate (BaSO4) powder.

For the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement, the as-prepared TNA and TNA-GO were served as the working electrodes, with a platinum foil as the counter electrode and a SCE (saturated calomel electrode) as the reference electrode. All EIS measurements were carried out in 0.5M Na2SO4 solution by using a three-electrode system on Zennium electrochemical workstation (Zahner, INC. Germany). The impedance spectra were recorded under an AC perturbation signal of 5mV over the frequency range of 0.01Hz to 100,000Hz at the initial potentials of working electrode materials. To further study the charge transfer behaviors of TNA and TNA-GO, EIS measurements under LED irradiation (TLS02, Zahner, INC. Germany) with a power 100mW/cm2 were also performed.

Photocurrent studies of TNA and TNA-GO on both the 1st day, and 61st day were performed in a photoelectrochemical quartz cell containing 0.5M Na2SO4 solution on the Zennium electrochemical workstation CIMPS system. In the three-electrode configuration, the TNA and TNA-GO sheets were working electrodes and, a Pt foil and a SCE were the counter electrode and reference electrode respectively. A LED lamp with a power 100mW/cm2 served as simulated visible light irradiation source. Between the photocurrent measurements on the 1st day and 61st day, both TNA and TNA-GO were stored at the ambient condition. As reference, the dark currents of TNA and TNA-GO were also measured.

Unlike the conventional optical filter approach to cut-off the UV light of the applied light source, we directly used a commercial LED lamp set together with Zennium electrochemical workstation as our experimental setup. The applied Zahner CIMPS system can accurately provide the visible light source in several certain wavelengths, and mixed white light (specification of instrument). In our photocurrent measurement tests, the mixed white light was applied, which does not emit UV light. Thus, all photocurrent measurements can be limited to light with a wavelength larger than 400nm.

Compared to the top-view SEM image of TNA in Fig. 2a, b showed the SEM image of a confirmed formation of TNA-GO. It can be seen from Fig. 2b that, after the LB assembly, TNA has been successfully covered by GO nanosheets. The chemical composition of the TNA and TNA-GO were both determined by EDX experiments (Fig. 2g and h), which demonstrated that the as-prepared TNA samples contained elements Ti and O while TNA-GO containing Ti, O and C. These EDX results also confirmed the GO assembly on TNA surface.

The phase structures of TNA and TNA-GO were analyzed by powder XRD patterns (Fig. 3 a). Facets (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116), (220), (215) and (303) of the anatase TiO2 were found in both TNA and TNA-GO samples (JCPDS No. 99-101-0957) at 25.33°, 37.83°, 48.08°, 53.93°, 55.11°, 62.74°, 68.82°, 70.35°, 75.12° and 82.76°, respectively. The peak at 2θ values of 40.2°, circled in Fig. 3a, matched the (101) crystal plane of Ti metal phase (JCPDS No. 99-101-0388), which were brought in by Ti substrate. Compared with TNA pattern, one more broad peak at around 2θ=11.5° showed up in TNA-GO pattern. This peak should be corresponding to a (002) facet diffraction of GO, which verified the existence of GO in the TNA-GO composite.

The consistence of TNA and TNA-GO composite were further investigated by Raman spectra (Fig. 3b). In Fig. 3b, four peaks were observed in TNA curve at around 144cm−1, 395cm−1, 512cm−1 and 639cm−1, matching four modes of TiO2 as Eg(1), B1g(1), A1g+B1g(2) and Eg(2) respectively, while the peaks at 144cm−1 being the highest. These four peaks can also be seen in the Raman curve of TNA-GO composite, but the intensity of the peaks were largely reduced. This decreased intensity was likely associated with a shielding effect of GO nanosheets on TNA, because the Raman test could only reflect the surface information of tested materials based on its surface-enhanced scattering mechanism [23]. Meanwhile, the typical peaks at around 1345cm−1 and 1580cm−1 of GO were found in TNA-GO, commonly corresponding to the D band of the sp3 defects and the G band of sp2 plane vibrations. Generally, the intensity ratio of D band and G band (ID/IG) represents defects in graphene. With an ID/IG value of 2.1, the GO on TNA-GO’s surface was found to be well oxidized from graphite.

XPS characterizations were further carried out to investigate TNA and TNA-GO composite (Fig. 4 ). The entire XPS survey of TNA and TNA-GO was shown as Fig. 4a, while the high-resolution spectra of C 1s and O 1s as Fig. 4b and c. In Fig. 4a, C 1s, Ti 2p and O 1s peaks at around their corresponding binding energies, as 284.5eV, 458eV and 531eV were exhibited. It can be easily determined from the curves that the intensity of Ti 2p and O 1s peaks in TNA and TNA-GO composite were about the same, while the C 1s peak intensity of TNA being markedly smaller than the TNA-GO composite. Technically, no C 1s peak should show up in TNA XPS survey. This small C 1s peak of TNA was most likely due to the residual ethylene glycol on its surface which may not have been totally washed out after the anodization synthesis. To have a closer look on the C 1s core level analysis of TNA and TNA-GO composite (Fig. 3b), three peaks as CC, CO and CO with corresponding banding energies at around 284.8eV, 286.3eV and 288.2eV [24] were observed in TNA-GO composite, while only two peaks as CC (284.6eV) [25] and COH (286.9eV) [26] showing up in TNA. In the O 1s core level spectrum, two peaks around 530.5eV and 532.7eV, corresponding to bulk oxygen (O2−) in TiO and in HO/CO covalent bond respectively, could be found in TNA-GO composite [24], while only one peak of TiO at around 529.8eV showed up in TNA. The reason why the energy of TiO drifted increased a little in TNA-GO was likely due to the fact that GO made the lattice of TNA more compacted. Thus, the core level analyses of C 1s and O 1s further confirmed the GO in TNA-GO composite. Moreover, the increasing binding energy of the internal electron (1s orbital electron) of O would reversely lead a relax of O neutron to hold its external valance electrons, and thus the excitation of electron in TNA/GO composite became easier and response to a longer-wavelength energy was likely. Therefore, what we found was the shift of the binding energy in O1s to a larger value in TNA/GO composite likely revealed the change of its band gap to be smaller.

It is well known that TiO2, as a photosensitized semiconductor, has a large band gap and good response to UV light. However, the UV spectra only accounts for about 3–5% of the total solar energy. Thus, to make a better use of solar energy, it is necessary to expand the photo-responsive spectra of TiO2 materials from the UV light to the visible light. This is also the major goal of our current studies.

The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) and the corresponding relationship between (Ahν)1/2 and hν were performed to examine the photocatalytic activities of TNA and TNA-GO composite. As shown in Fig. 5 a, both TNA and TNA-GO composite responded predominantly to UV light. With direct band gap, the band gap of TNA and TNA-GO composite can be determined by the Kubelka-Munk equation as the intercepts in Fig. 5b. Respectively, the band gap of TNA and TNA-GO composite were about 3.0eV (corresponding to wavelength of ∼400nm, UV light range) and 2.8eV (corresponding to wavelength of ∼450nm, visible light range). Thus, the band gap of TNA-GO was narrowed down 0.2eV compared to TNA itself. Similar results were also found by other groups [18]j,k,n,o. By having narrower band gap, the photo-response range of TNA can then change from UV to visible light, which also enlarges the utilization range of natural sun light.

Here, an up-conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) effect of GO may provide a synergic explanation to this improvement of light absorption. According to published studies, UCPL is a phenomenon that the photon energy of emission is higher than that of excitation, which has been widely reported with solid-state materials doped with rare-earth elements, semiconductors with heterostructures, quantum dots, etc [27]. Recently, normal PL as well as UCPL features of carbon quantum dots, graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide quantum dots have been well characterized [18]m, [28,29]. By mentioning RG quantum dots, it does not only refer to the actual dots with size smaller than 10nm, but also the graphene sheets with graphene oxide quantum islands on it, both being in a nano-thickness [30]. In this study, the GO on TNA can be seen as a quantum graphene structure material. Normally, the visible light with wavelength larger than 420nm is not be able to be used by TiO2 materials alone (The absorbable light wavelength limitation of the rutile TiO2 is 414nm, while the absorbable light wavelength limitation of the anatase TiO2 is 388nm [28]b). However, the visible light within this range can excite GO and transfer to the light with shorter wavelength, which is then able to be utilized by TiO2. Thus, the absorbable light wavelength limitation is accordingly changed.

The charge carriers’ migration behaviors of TNA and TNA-GO composite were also explored by measuring EIS. As shown in the –Nyquist plots (Fig. 5c), the radii of the quadrants at high frequencies range (closer to the origin) can reflect charge transfer resistance, and the material with larger radius is supposed to have relatively larger resistance. From Fig. 5c it can be determined that the radius of TNA’s quadrant was marginally larger than the radius of TNA-GO’s, which should be according to the existence of GO on the surface of TNA decreasing the charge transfer resistance across the solid-liquid junction. To further study the charge transfer behaviors of TNA and TNA-GO composite, we also performed similar EIS measurements under LED irradiation (Fig. 5d). As shown in Fig. 5d, when TNA and TNA-GO composite were under irradiation (100mW/cm2), both charge transfer resistances decreased, and the one of TNA-GO composite decreased more than the one of TNA. The reason of this falling is likely the influence of the photocurrent produced by TNA and TNA-GO composite under irradiation conditions.

To test the visible light photoresponsive activities of TNA and TNA-GO composite, the transient photocurrent responses measurements with 50s visible light on-off cycles were also performed of both samples (Fig. 5e and f). As shown in Fig. 5e, while no significant photocurrent was observed in the dark, both TNA and TNA-GO electrodes had both fairly stable photocurrent response under subsequent repetitive visible light irradiation (LED light source, λ>400nm). To compare these two samples, the TNA-GO electrode showed a much better photocurrent response with the photocurrent density of 32μAcm−2, which is more than two times higher than bare TNA electrode (approximately 12μAcm−2). The result can be also noticed that both TNA and TNA-GO electrodes suffered a gradually increase of photocurrent density during photocurrent testing, which should be due to the fact that a certain heat created by light irritation can help speeding up electron movement in the system and then increasing the photocurrent as a result. To test the stability of as-prepared TNA and TNA-GO sample, the same transient photocurrent responses measurements were tested again after 2-month (Fig. 5f). In this subsequent test, the photosensitivity of both TNA and TNA-GO was retained at about the same value (∼31μAcm−2) as 60days ago. Moreover, compared to the 1st day sample, the photosensitivity of after-2-month day TNA-GO sample showed more consistent, unvarying, response steadiness. Dark currents of both TNA and TNA-GO were also performed as contrasted reference (Fig. 5g). The dark currents of TNA and TNA-GO resulted in being both relatively small and steady (TNA∼1μAcm−2, TNA-GO∼2μAcm−2).

The mechanism of the TNA-GO enhanced photoresponse, compared to TNA, can be schemed as: 1) The band gap of TNA-GO is narrower than TNA, which gives TNA-GO a better response to visible light compared to TNA. 2) When TNA was excited by light irridation, the electrons in the valence band of TiO2 can jump into its conduct band and the photogenerated electron–hole pairs are then formed. Since GO has a relatively smaller band gap and a larger work function, a Schottky junction will be formed between TNA and GO. Owing to the Schottky junction, electrons in the conduct band of TNA can be transferred to GO and the holes stay in the valence band of TNA, which will certainly lead to efficient charges’ separation [31]. Thus, by having Schottky junction, the rate of recombination of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs decreases significantly, which then consequently enhances the photocurrent.

The hydrophilic ability of TNA and TNA-GO were also tested by our self-made goniometer camera. Compared to TNA (Fig. 5h), the contact angle of water on TNA-GO composite (Fig. 5i) was greatly reduced. This difference should be attributed to the fact that the participation of GO brought some hydroxyl groups on TNA’ surfaces, and these aqueous-favoring hydroxyl groups made TNA-GO sample more hydrophilic.

From all the results above, highly ordered TNA sample was synthesized by utilizing anodic oxidation method. After LB assembly, GO nanosheets have been successfully fabricated on the TNA’s surface. This nanostructure is uniform and is able to be formed on large-scale substrates. Though the TNA-GO layer is very thin compared to its titanium substrate, the absorption range of light has had a red-shift of 0.2eV, which means more sunlight with longer wavelength can be used by applying this composite to photocatalyst. Meanwhile, compared to TNA, the resistance of TNA-GO composite decreased marginally, especially under an irradiation condition. The hydrophilicity of TNA-GO was greatly improved as compared to TNA. More thorough photocatalytic properties tests and characterizations of this LB assembled TNA-GO composite will be performed in further study.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the 2014-2015 NSF RSP EPSCoR program and 2015-2017 NSF REG & RSP EPSCoR program (the National Science Foundation under Cooperative Agreement No. 1355438), and the support from the NSF-CHE-MRI under the Award ID of 1338072. Authors also extend our acknowledgement to partial support by the U.S. Department of Agriculture (5040-12630-004-00D). Additionally, the authors also very much appreciate the help from Dr. Sanju Gupta (sanju.gupta@wku.edu) - Raman tests, Dr. John Andersland (john.andersland@wku.edu) - electron microscope training, Dr. Hanjing Tian (htian@mail.wvu.edu) - XPS tests, and Mr. Craig Clark (william.clark927@topper.wku.edu) - proofreading.
